title: 2 3 Gradient and directional derivative
body:
2.3 GRADIENT AND DIRECTIONAL DERIVATIVE

       Given a scalar field ПҶ(x, y, z), the gradient of ПҶ is a vector field given by

                           вҲӮПҶ    вҲӮПҶ    вҲӮПҶ
         вҲҮПҶ = grad ПҶ =        i+    j+    k.               grad : scalar вҶ’ vector
                           вҲӮx    вҲӮy    вҲӮz

   The gradient of ПҶ at the point P , вҲҮПҶ|P , is a vector pointing in the direction of
 maximum increase with the rate of increase given by the magnitude of вҲҮПҶ|P . The
           direction and rate of maximum decrease is given by вҲ’вҲҮПҶ|P .

  The rate of change of ПҶ at the point P in the direction of the vector b is given by the
                                 directional derivative
                                          вҲӮПҶ        вҲӮПҶ        вҲӮПҶ
                         (вҲҮПҶ|P ) В· bМӮ =        b1 +      b2 +      b3
                                          вҲӮx P      вҲӮy P      вҲӮz P

             where bМӮ = b1 i + b2 j + b3 k is the unit vector in the direction of b.


Given a scalar field, such as the temperature at every point in the room, we would like to
be able to measure how the scalar field changes with position. This information is given
by the gradient of the scalar field. At a point P , the gradient tells you the direction and
magnitude of maximum increase of the scalar field. If we want to know the rate of change
of ПҶ in a particular direction, we use the directional derivative.

Notice that gradient takes a scalar field and gives back a vector field вҖ” for every point in
space, it gives a vector pointing in the direction that ПҶ is increasing fastest, with the rate
of increase given by the magnitude of the vector. We will return to the subject of vector
fields in more detail in the next topic.

Example 1 Let ПҶ(x, y, z) = x2 y 3 z + 2y       be a scalar field. Calculate grad(ПҶ).




                                                                        пЈ«              пЈ¶
                                                                            2xy 3 z
                              F 2xy 3 zi + (3x2 y 2 z + 2)j + x2 y 3 k = пЈӯ3x2 y 2 z + 2пЈё   F
                                                                             x2 y 3

                                               1
 The directional derivative of ПҶ at the point P in the direction of the unit vector bМӮ is given
by the dot product of bМӮ with вҲҮПҶ|P . Recall that the dot product of two vectors is

       u = u1 i + u2 j + u3 k,   v = v1 i + v2 j + v3 k,   u В· v = u1 v1 + u2 v2 + u3 v3 .

It is important to make sure that the direction vector bМӮ is a unit vector!


Example 2 Calculate the directional derivative of the temperature field ПҶ = x2 yz in
the direction i + 2j + 2k at the point P (вҲ’1, 1, 3).




                                                                                         2
                                                                                   F     3
                                                                                             F


This means that if you start at the point P (вҲ’1, 1, 3) and move in the direction i + 2j + 2k
then the instantaneous rate of change of temperature is equal to 32 .

Notice that the directional derivative just gives us back a number : the rate of change of
ПҶ at a point P in the direction b.


                                                2
 We can now prove that the directional derivative does indeed give us the rate of change
of a scalar field ПҶ(x, y, z) in the direction bМӮ. To do this, we will first remind ourselves how
to define a straight line path in three dimensions.

Example 3 Find a parametric vector equation for the line passing through the points
P (3, 1, 2) and Q(4, 4, 3).




                                                                 пЈ«     пЈ¶ пЈ«       пЈ¶
                                                                  x(t)     3+t
                                                            F    пЈӯy(t)пЈё = пЈӯ1 + 3tпЈё         F
                                                                  z(t)     2+t

                                               3
 Proof of formula: We will prove the result in three dimensions. The argument in other
dimensions is similar.

We want to calculate the rate of change of ПҶ in the direction of the unit vector bМӮ, given
by                                        пЈ«    пЈ¶
                                            b1
                                     bМӮ = пЈӯ b2 пЈё .
                                            b3
First we define the straight-line path from a point P (x0 , y0 , z0 ) in the direction of bМӮ,
                     пЈ«        пЈ¶ пЈ«         пЈ¶ пЈ«       пЈ¶        пЈ«          пЈ¶
                        x(s)          x0        b1                b1 s
                     пЈӯ y(s) пЈё = пЈӯ y0 пЈё + пЈӯ b2 пЈё s = пЈӯ b2 s пЈё ,
                        z(s)          z0        b3                b3 s

where s is the distance from the point P .




Now, ПҶ(x, y, x) depends upon x, y and z which in turn depend upon s. Hence, via the
chain rule:
                                                       пЈ« вҲӮПҶ пЈ¶
                                                 пЈ¬ вҲӮx пЈ·
                                                 пЈ¬    пЈ· пЈ«     пЈ¶
                                                 пЈ¬    пЈ·
                                                 пЈ¬ вҲӮПҶ пЈ·   b 1
dПҶ   вҲӮПҶ вҲӮx вҲӮПҶ вҲӮy вҲӮПҶ вҲӮz   вҲӮПҶ      вҲӮПҶ      вҲӮПҶ
                                                 пЈ¬ вҲӮy пЈ· В·
   =      +     +      =    b1 +    b2 +    b3 = пЈ¬    пЈ· пЈӯ b2 пЈё = (вҲҮПҶ) В· bМӮ
ds   вҲӮx вҲӮs вҲӮy вҲӮs вҲӮz вҲӮs   вҲӮx      вҲӮy      вҲӮz      пЈ¬
                                                 пЈ¬
                                                      пЈ·
                                                      пЈ·   b3
                                                   вҲӮПҶ
                                                 пЈӯ    пЈё
                                                   вҲӮz
as required.                                                                                    F

                                                4
 With the directional derivative we can calculate the rate of change of a scalar field in any
direction. But which direction should we move in to increase the scalar field as quickly as
possible? In other words, which direction b gives the maximum directional derivative?

In first year you learned that the dot product of u and v could be written as

                                           u В· v = |u||v| cos Оё,

where Оё is the angle between the two vectors. So the directional derivative is

                                   (вҲҮПҶ|P ) В· bМӮ = |вҲҮПҶ|P ||bМӮ| cos Оё,

where Оё is now the angle between |вҲҮПҶ|P | and bМӮ. But the gradient is fixed and |bМӮ| = 1
because bМӮ is a unit vector. So the maximum value of the directional derivative will occur
when cos Оё = 1, that is, when Оё = 0 and вҲҮПҶ|P and bМӮ point in the same direction.

So the direction that maximises the directional derivative is just the unit vector pointing
in the same direction as вҲҮПҶ|P . WhatвҖҷs more, the magnitude of the maximum rate of
change is given by |вҲҮПҶ|P |. This means that the gradient of a scalar field at the point P
can be understood as a vector pointing in the direction of maximum increase of ПҶ and with
magnitude equal to the rate of maximum increase. Likewise, the direction and magnitude
of maximum decrease of ПҶ at the point P is given by вҲ’вҲҮПҶ|P .

                                                                       2
Example 4 Consider the scalar field ПҶ(x, y, z) = x2 z+2y 2 вҲ’yez . What is the magnitude
and direction of the maximum rate of change of ПҶ at P (1, 2, 0)?




                                                                                       вҲҡ
                                                                           F 7j + k,    50 F
  1
      You can now do Q 54, 56, 57 and 58
